title: What Netflix's Documentary Didn't Tell You

chunks.title: Criticism of Vegan Study
chunks.summary: The speaker critiques a Netflix documentary on a study comparing vegan and omnivore diets, highlighting that the study's vegan twin participants lost more weight due to being given fewer calories. Furthermore, they point out that the vegan group lost muscle mass and presented misleading results regarding health benefits.
chunks.transcript: what do I think of the recent documentary on Netflix you are what you eat here are my thoughts for starters directed by a vegan the main research scientist is a vegan and runs the Stanford plant-based initiative which is funded by beyond meat a plant-based Meat Company to suggest that this is a sort of vegan propaganda piece is likely so what did the study actually find in this study they took 22 sets of twins in each twin pair one twin was given a vegan diet and the other twin was given an omnivore diet the researchers are very proud of the fact that the vegan twins lost more weight than the omnivore twins but if you dig more into what actually happened you see that the researchers provided the vegan twins with less calories well that will result in more weight loss and in the examples of dexus scans shown in the documentary the vegan twins lost significantly more muscle mass than the omnivores in many cases resulting in a higher body fat percentage at the end of the study in fact many of the omnivores didn't lose any muscle mass they gained muscle mass lost a little bit of fat but that resulted in not looking like they lost as much weight so the authors were really not forthcoming with this data in fact I looked through the paper the supplemental materials everything I could find and the individual dexas scan data regarding weight loss fat loss versus muscle mass loss for every one of those 22 sets of twins is not presented anywhere you can't find it what is presented is the notion that the vegans lost more weight and because of that weight loss they had an improve in insulin sensitivity but we also know that you can give someone a reduced calorie diet of anything including Twinkies which has been done and that results in Improvement in insulin sensitivity so just because there was a weight loss and a corresponding Improvement in insulin sensitivity doesn't mean that the diet was Superior especially when the researchers specifically gave the vegan arm less calories than the omnivores in terms of nutrient levels in the study the story doesn't look very good for the vegans

chunks.title: Nutrient Deficiencies in Vegan Diets
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses how the vegan participants in the study experienced declines in essential nutrients like B12 and suggests that had more nutrients been tested, the deficiencies in the vegan group would have been apparent. They argue that vegan diets may lack critical micronutrients for optimal health.
chunks.transcript: predictably the vegan group in the study had levels of B12 that went down while the omnivore group saw levels of B12 go up that was really one of the only nutrients they checked in the study but if they checked things like vitamin K2 levels creatine levels in the muscle Coline levels any of the nutrients that are predominant found in animal Foods they would almost have certainly seen that many nutrients essential for optimal human health also went down in the vegan group but the researchers didn't look at it so it didn't happen right just turned a blind eye to the fact that the vegan diet is a little bit micronutrient deficient in terms of satisfaction the vegan group consistently rated their meals with less satisfaction than the omnivore group we know that long-term adherence to vegan diets is poor corroborating this finding and we know that longterm the majority of vegans return to eating meat with the majority of those people citing health problems as the reason that they reintroduce meat into their diet so that's not a great thing either but the researchers don't really tell us about that prominently another finding that the researchers are proud of in this study is that the vegan group had lower levels of LDL cholesterol at the end of

chunks.title: Satisfaction and Long-term Adherence
chunks.summary: It is noted that the vegan group rated their meal satisfaction lower than the omnivores and that research shows many vegans eventually return to eating meat due to health issues. The speaker emphasizes the poor long-term adherence to vegan diets.
chunks.transcript: the study but one thing that we know consistently in the science is that animal fats like butter or fats on steaks will lead to a little bit of a rise in the LDL level and when you have more seed oils you lower your LDL level if you look more in the details of this at least in the studies that have been done in the medical literature a lowering of the LDL with increased polyunsaturated plant fats is also accompanied by an increase in oxidized LDL and LP ltil A lipoprotein ltil a and those are strong risk factors for an increase in cardiovascular disease risk now the researchers in this study conveniently didn't check oxidized LDL or LP little a in these patients they only looked at LDL so predictably LDL goes up a little bit there are a lot of reasons this can happen the details here are not super important but I strongly believe that an increase in LDL cholesterol from eating healthy things like well- raised red meat butter Etc is not going to increase cardiovascular risk because we know that those foods are not going to worsen metabolic dysfunction that's a subject for a much longer video but the notion that we should be impressed by a vegan diet lowering LDL is a little bit misleading

chunks.title: Misleading LDL Cholesterol Findings
chunks.summary: The discussion focuses on the claim that vegan diets lower LDL cholesterol, highlighting how low LDL levels can be misleading without considering oxidized LDL and other factors. The speaker argues that the increase in LDL from animal fats does not correlate with higher cardiovascular risk.
chunks.transcript: again one of the parts in this documentary that was most frustrating to me was when they had a regenerative farmer cooking kale and saying that he

chunks.title: Regenerative Agriculture Explained
chunks.summary: The speaker argues that regenerative agriculture practices with ruminants are carbon negative and beneficial for soil health, countering claims in the documentary that these practices are not scalable. They emphasize that animal waste contributes to soil nutrient regeneration.
chunks.transcript: was eating less regenerative beef or that regenerative agriculture of animals like cows was not scalable this is crazy first things first regenerative agriculture of ruminants like cows is clearly carbon negative meaning that when you raise a cow on grass for its whole life with rotational grazing like they do at White Oak pastures or other regenerative Farms it has been shown repeatedly through life cycle analysis that this sequesters more carbon into the soil than is actually released in the production of that beef the same cannot be said for plant-based Meats which are carbon positive nor can it be said for cell-based Meats there's clear research showing that cell-based meats are very carbon positive and will represent much more greenhouse gas emissions than actual beef in equivalent amounts I'm not sure how that makes any sense or why people are so excited about cell-based Meats but regardless let's talk about regenerative agriculture and scalability there is a large program in the United States called the conservation Reserve program this program pays Farmers to let their fields lay fallow to not raise any plants on their fields so that the fields can actually regenerate the nutrients in them and they say that it takes about 50 years for a plot of land that has been stripped of nutrients by monocrop agriculture of plants to actually be able to be usable for plant agriculture again well the best way to improve the quality of soil much more quickly than 50 years is to put animals on that land the poop and peeing of the animals brings more nutrients to the soil and regenerates the land much more quickly than it does without animals on it so if you want to have healthy land to grow plants you can do that by placing animals on the land and letting them eat the grass live like they always have for millions of years as ruminants in the United States or around the world that is the way to protect our soils and to protect our lands now the scalability of that is only based on consumer demand and propaganda misleading documentaries like this don't do any good to helping people understand the power of regenerative agriculture so this kind of stuff really bothers me guys scalability of regenerative agriculture is absolutely possible and it's based on whether or not you value it whether you

chunks.title: Saturated Fat Misconceptions
chunks.summary: Criticism is directed at claims that saturated fat from animals causes cardiovascular issues. The speaker points to research showing no negative cardiovascular risk associated with saturated fats, arguing that the information presented in the documentary is misleading.
chunks.transcript: purchase it in the store and whether you support your local farmers there's plenty of land to do this there are hundreds of thousands of acres in the conservation Reserve program that we could be putting cattle on right now feed Lots which are clustered animal feeding operations could be turned into regenerative agriculture if that land is allowed to regenerate and people buy less feed lot beef and more grass-fed grass-finished regeneratively raised beef so it's totally possible but advocates for plant-based diets like to straw man this type of thing to make it seem like we can't raise enough cows when in fact the ability to expand Regin of Agriculture is vast it also grinds my gears when the people in this documentary State matter of factly that saturated fat from animals clogs your arteries when there are large meta analyses of trials with saturated fat clearly showing that there is no negative cardiio vascular risk with eating more saturated fat in your diet and there are multiple studies looking at saturated fats in Dairy associating that saturated fat with lower levels of liver fat and improved cardiovascular outcomes time and time again I don't even know how anyone could say that saturated fat from animals clogs your arteries in this day and age there's so much research to suggest that is not the case that this just makes me a little crazy and it's just clear propaganda at this point if you're interested in the greenhouse gas emissions related to cattle it's important to consider that EPA data from 2016 and 2021 with regard

chunks.title: Greenhouse Gas Emissions in Agriculture
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses greenhouse gas emissions related to cattle, presenting data that suggests their contribution is significantly lower than that from plant agriculture. They argue against the notion that reducing beef consumption would mitigate greenhouse gases effectively.
chunks.transcript: to ruminant agriculture the 2021 analysis by the EPA suggested that about 10% of greenhouse gases in the United States were related to the agriculture industry and about half of that comes from animals and about half of that comes from cows so something around 2.5% of greenhouse gas emissions in the United States come from cattle and 2.5% of greenhouse gas emissions come from chickens pork turkey other things and and around 5% of greenhouse gas emissions come from plant agriculture because when you're tilling the soil to do monocrop agriculture that releases carbon dioxide into the air and the atmosphere as well so the vegan perspective that argues we should eliminate all cattle or significantly reduce the amount of beef on the planet to lower greenhouse gases doesn't make sense to me because I'm not sure what you would replace that beef with if agriculture from Plants contributes even more greenhouse gases than cattle do based the 2021 EPA analysis of greenhouse gases in the United States Riddle Me That vegans I don't know I'm waiting to hear about it doesn't make

chunks.title: Ethical Considerations of Veganism
chunks.summary: Addressing the ethics of veganism, the speaker notes that monocrop agriculture may kill more animals than animal agriculture does. They argue that animal foods are nutrient-dense and can be produced responsibly and ethically.
chunks.transcript: sense to me and many people will argue that plant agriculture goes to feed the cows but when you actually look at the data only about 133% of what ruminant animals cows eat is this is

chunks.title: Final Thoughts on the Documentary
chunks.summary: The speaker concludes that the Netflix documentary is misinformative and potentially harmful, suggesting that it promotes a vegan agenda that might mislead viewers about nutrient needs. They express concern over the implications for those following a vegan diet.
chunks.transcript: misleading people and harming them in the long run I believe that the people behind the study are very intelligent and well-intentioned but the execution here is poor and this is propaganda
